question: Should Public College Be Tuition-Free?
agent1_stance:
Your stance:
You support tuition-free college.
You believe tuition-free college would help decrease student debt, as student loan debt rose over 240 percent between 2006 and 2024 and eliminating tuition costs would significantly reduce borrowing burdens that take an average of 20 years to repay.
You believe our society and economy have benefited from tuition-free college in the past, as the G.I. Bill enabled 2.2 million veterans to earn degrees, generating $35.6 billion over 35 years with a return of $6.90 for every dollar spent.
You believe everyone deserves the opportunity to get a college education, as at least three-quarters of the fastest-growing occupations now call for education beyond high school, yet students from low- and moderate-income families are unable to afford about 95 percent of American colleges.
agent2_stance:
Your stance:
You oppose tuition-free college.
You believe tuition-free college is not free, and students would still have large debts, as tuition represents only 30-75% of total college costs and beyond tuition, students face $18,300 per year in fees, room and board, books, and other necessary expenses.
You believe taxpayers would spend billions to subsidize tuition, while other college costs would remain high, as free college programs would require tax increases and force states to move existing resources away from other state priorities like health care, prisons, roads and K-12 education.
You believe tuition-free college attracts students unsuited for higher education, wasting public funds, as under California's fee waiver program, completion rates remained low despite free enrollment.
